nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, clear tropical reefs over coral or rocky bottoms; perches and hops among rockwork.

diet.notes: Grazes on microalgae across rockwork. Diet should be supplemented with marine algae, nori, spirulina, and algae-based prepared foods 2–3 times daily, particularly if natural algae growth is limited.

goodWith: hardy reef fish that do not resemble blennies

summary: Caribbean reef blenny with bright red lips and a blunt, horse-like head. Perches and hops over rockwork grazing on algae; generally territorial and best kept singly.
careNotes: Generally considered an algae-grazing 'utility' fish for established reef tanks with substantial live rock. Tends to be territorial — particularly toward other blennies, gobies, and dartfish — and is best kept as the only blenny in the system unless paired in a larger tank. Has been reported to nip at small-polyped stony corals and clam mantles, so reef-safety is best described as 'with caution'. Like most blennies, has been known to jump from open tanks; a tight-fitting lid is generally advised. Sources disagree substantially on minimum tank size: LiveAquaria lists 30 US gal (~114 L), Quality Marine lists 70 US gal (~265 L), Fishkeeping World cites 20–29 US gal, and saltwaterfish.com lists 75 US gal. A cautious 150 L (~40 US gal) is used here, reflecting the species' territoriality, adult body length (around 12 cm), and active hopping/perching behavior; reviewers may want to revisit this. LiveAquaria sells this species under the older name *Ophioblennius atlanticus*, but FishBase now treats Caribbean populations as a distinct species, *O. macclurei* (Silvester, 1915); *O. atlanticus* sensu stricto is the East Atlantic population. RESOLVED 2026-06-03: Tank size concern resolved. Cross-checked FishBase (max 12.2 cm TL confirmed, no tank size given), Wikipedia (taxonomic only, no husbandry), LiveAquaria (30 US gal, ~114 L), and Quality Marine (70 US gal, ~265 L). The Fishkeeping World 20–29 gal figure is discounted as an outlier likely reflecting a different or smaller species. Weighting the professional trade recommendation (Quality Marine, 70 US gal) and the species' documented high conspecific aggression, active perching behavior, and 12 cm adult size, minVolumeLiters revised upward from 150 L to 200 L (~53 US gal) — the next step on the approved values list — and minLengthCm updated from 90 to 100 cm to match. This sits between LiveAquaria's minimum and Quality Marine's recommendation and is a defensible cautious-but-not-extreme figure. No evidence of sex change found in any source consulted; fish.sexChange remains null.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing; eggs are demersal and adhesive, attached to a substrate. Captive breeding is not commonly reported.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Listed under the legacy name Ophioblennius atlanticus.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Assigned secondary returned HTTP 403; cross-verification fell through to FishBase, Quality Marine, and Fishkeeping World.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Ophioblennius macclurei, Silvester 1915), family Blenniidae, max length 12.2 cm TL, temperature 26.2–28.2°C wild, Caribbean/Western Central Atlantic range, territorial, oviparous with distinct pairing.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Recommends a 70 US gal minimum (~265 L) and describes the species as very aggressive/territorial toward smaller tankmates — the high end of the source spread.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Care summary aggregated via web search snippet (page itself 404'd): 10–13 cm adult length, 22–26°C, pH 8.0–8.5, SG 1.020–1.026, algae-grazing diet, jumping risk.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing; eggs demersal and adhesive. Captive breeding is uncommon and not well documented for the home aquarium.
